title: Chapter 34: The Crystal Tower
content: The Crystal Tower caught the morning light and shattered it into a thousand prismatic fragments that scattered across the neighboring rooftops like fallen stars. Up close, the building was even more surreal — its facade wasn't glass at all, but some kind of crystalline polymer that shifted color with the angle of the sun, cycling from deep amber to pale gold to blinding white as the group approached from the west.
It was, by any measure, the most beautiful building Marcus had ever seen. And it was utterly silent.

No movement in the lobby. No signs of habitation in the floors visible from the adjacent rooftop. The broad front doors were closed and barricaded from inside — he could see the edge of furniture pressed against the glass, cast in rainbow light. The lower floors had been systematically cleared: no zombies, no raiders, no people at all. Just emptiness, sealed and waiting.

Junko stopped at the edge of the connecting roof — a department store three stories shorter than the Crystal Tower's base, with a gap of maybe four meters between them. She studied the building with an expression Marcus couldn't read.
"I've never been inside," she said. "Never needed to. The ground floor's been sealed since week two, and the woman upstairs doesn't welcome visitors."
"How does she eat?" Elena asked.

"She doesn't. Not much." Junko's jaw tightened. "I've watched from this roof — she comes out on the penthouse balcony sometimes. Thinner every week. She's starving and she won't leave and she won't ask for help."
Marcus was about to ask why when Sera's hand closed on his arm like a vice.

"There's someone alive in there," Sera said. Her silver eyes were unfocused — the tell that meant she was extending her senses, pushing the Bond's perception to its limit. "I can feel a heartbeat. One. Strong but stressed. Elevated cortisol — fight-or-flight response, sustained."
"The penthouse?" Marcus asked.
"The penthouse. Three — no, four floors up from the top." Sera's brow furrowed. "She knows we're here. Her heart rate just spiked."

**[TARGET DETECTED: Vivienne Duchamps (C007)]**
**[Classification: Human Survivor — Fortified Position]**
**[Threat Level: HIGH]**
**[Romance Target: YES]**
**[Active Quest: The Proud Heart]**
**[Objective: Make Vivienne Duchamps ask for help. Not demand. Ask.]**
**[Warning: Target Pride Barrier at 98%. Approach with extreme caution — emotional rejection probability: 94%]**
**[Reward: Vivienne Affection +25, Item: Shortwave Radio Array]**

The notification burned across Marcus's vision, amber and insistent. A new romance target. A new quest. A woman so proud she was starving to death in a crystal tower rather than admit she needed anything from anyone.
"System quest," he told the group. "Pride Barrier. Ninety-eight percent. She won't accept help — I have to get her to *ask* for it."
"That's impossible," Elena said flatly. "You can't make a drowning person ask to be saved. They just drown."
"Then I have to figure out how to make her want to not drown." Marcus studied the tower. "Junko, you said the rooftop route forks past here. We need to go through?"
"Through or around. Around means descending and crossing at street level — three blocks of exposed ground with Crane's snipers." Junko's expression was unreadable. "Through means dealing with her."

Marcus looked at Sera. Through the Bond, he felt her reaction — complex, layered. The familiar spike of jealousy, sharp and hot, that came with every new romance target. The logical override that kept it contained. And beneath both, something unexpected: *curiosity*. Sera was curious about this woman who'd chosen isolation over dependence, who'd built walls so high she was dying behind them.

"I can feel her loneliness," Sera said quietly. "Even from here. Her heartbeat is... the rhythm of someone who's been alone so long they've forgotten what company sounds like." A pause. "I know that rhythm."
Marcus squeezed her hand. "Elena, you're with me. We go up. Sera, Junko — hold this position, watch for patrols. If anything moves on the street, signal."
"Marcus—"

"Sera." He met her silver eyes. "Her Pride Barrier is ninety-eight percent. She'll see a zombie queen as a threat, not a guest. Elena's human, and she's a medic. If this woman is starving, she needs medical assessment, not intimidation."

The logic was sound. Through the Bond, he felt Sera fight it — the instinct to stay close, to protect, to be present whenever a new thread formed between Marcus and another woman. The jealousy wasn't petty. It was existential, as real as the Eternal Bond itself, and Marcus felt every pulse of it like a second heartbeat.
But Sera was also a CEO. She understood resource allocation. She understood that sending the right person for the job wasn't abandonment — it was strategy.
"Two minutes," Sera said. "If you're not out in two minutes, I'm coming in regardless of her pride barrier."
"Two minutes," he agreed.

The Crystal Tower's fire escape was intact — a steel ladder bolted to the eastern face, hidden from street view by the building's crystalline overhangs. Marcus went up first, the rungs cold beneath his grip, Elena behind him with her medical bag slung across her chest.

The climb was eight floors. By the sixth, Marcus's arms were screaming. By the eighth, he could see the penthouse — a broad terrace walled in glass, the curtains drawn, a single chair visible through the gap.
He pulled himself onto the landing and found the door.

It was a beautiful door — hardwood with brass fittings, the kind of artisanal entrance that probably cost more than his entire pre-apocalypse apartment. It opened onto a kitchen that was all marble and stainless steel and absolute emptiness. No food on the counters. No dishes in the sink. Just clean surfaces and dead silence and the faint smell of dust.

Elena stepped in behind him and did what Elena always did: she assessed. "No generator hum. No water running. She's been without power and plumbing since the grid went down." She opened the refrigerator — empty, door propped slightly ajar to prevent mold. "Nothing. Not even condiment packets."

They moved through the penthouse in tactical formation, which was a generous way of describing Marcus jumping at shadows while Elena took notes. The living room was pristine — leather furniture, abstract art, a grand piano in the corner with sheet music still on the stand. A Chopin nocturne, half-played. The dining room had been converted into a barricade: the table pushed against the front door, chairs stacked on top, everything arranged with the methodical care of someone who treated survival as an aesthetic problem.
The bedroom was empty. The bathroom was empty. The study—
Marcus stopped.

The study was enormous — floor-to-ceiling windows on two walls, a massive mahogany desk, bookshelves lined with volumes in French and English and something that might have been medieval Latin. And standing in the center of the room, lit from behind by the prismatic light of her impossible tower, was Vivienne Duchamps.

She was tall. Taller than Marcus, even without the heeled boots she wore — black leather, scuffed but polished, the kind of boots that cost a thousand dollars before the world ended and still looked like they cost a thousand dollars now. Her hair was dark auburn, pulled back in a severe chignon that emphasized cheekbones sharp enough to cut. Her dress was black, simple, immaculate — not a survival outfit, a *statement*. She had chosen her clothes this morning with the same deliberate intentionality she'd chosen her barricades.

And in her right hand, held with the easy familiarity of someone who'd spent years learning its weight, was a fencing épée. The blade caught the crystal light and threw a thin white line across the floor like a challenge.

**[TARGET ENGAGED: Vivienne Duchamps]**
**[Pride Barrier: 98%]**
**[Combat Probability: 76%]**
**[Note: Target is trained. French national fencing team, alternate, 2016. Do NOT underestimate.]**
French national fencing team. Of course. Marcus was staring at a starving woman who could probably run him through before he finished saying "I come in peace."

Vivienne's eyes were green — pale, fierce, the green of sea glass and old money. They swept over Marcus with the clinical assessment of someone cataloguing threats, lingered on Elena for half a second longer (the medical bag registered, filed, deprioritized), and returned to Marcus with the kind of focus that made him feel like a specimen on a dissection table.
"Leave." Her voice was low, accented, each word placed with the precision of a blade. "I have nothing worth stealing."

"We're not here to steal," Marcus said. He kept his hands visible — palms out, fingers spread. Non-threatening. He hoped. "My name is Marcus Chen. This is Elena Reyes. We're from a settlement called New Eden, about six miles west of here. We're passing through on our way to Vale Industries Tower, and—"

"I said *leave*." The épée rose three degrees. Not a threat — a *correction*. "I do not require assistance. I do not require company. I do not require whatever it is you are selling, Monsieur Chen. This is my home, and you are trespassing."

Elena stepped forward, and the épée tracked her instantly — the point shifting with the fluid grace of a weapon that had spent years in competitive hands. "Ms. Duchamps, I'm a nurse. Your color is off, you're underweight, and your pupils are dilated beyond what lighting conditions would explain—"
"Your medical assessment is neither requested nor welcome." Vivienne's jaw tightened. "I am *fine*."

She was not fine. Marcus could see it now — the tremor in her sword hand that she was suppressing through sheer will, the hollows beneath her cheekbones that no amount of aristocratic posture could conceal, the faint unsteadiness in her stance that came from days of insufficient food. She was dying. Slowly, beautifully, with perfect posture and an épée in her hand, but dying all the same.

He thought about the quest. *Make Vivienne Duchamps ask for help. Not demand. Ask.* Ninety-eight percent Pride Barrier. She would rather die — *was* rather dying — than admit vulnerability to a stranger.
So he didn't ask her to.
Instead, Marcus sat down.

Not dramatically, not carefully — he just dropped onto the nearest chair like he belonged there, like the penthouse of a starving duelist in a crystal tower was exactly where he'd planned to spend his morning. He leaned back, crossed his arms, and looked up at her.
"I'm not leaving," he said.
The épée blurred.

Marcus felt the wind of the blade before he registered the movement — a thrust so fast it should have opened his throat, stopping precisely three centimeters from his jugular. The point hovered there, gleaming, perfectly still in a hand that must have been exhausted but refused to show it.

"I could kill you," Vivienne said. Her voice hadn't changed. Her breathing hadn't changed. She was standing in her study with a blade at a stranger's throat and her composure was *perfect*, which was somehow more terrifying than if she'd been screaming.
"You could," Marcus agreed. His pulse was hammering but he kept his voice level. "But then you'd be alone again."

The words hit harder than the épée could have. He saw it — the flicker. The tiniest crack in the porcelain mask, there and gone in the space of a heartbeat. Not weakness. Not surrender. Just the involuntary acknowledgment that he'd touched the one nerve she couldn't armor.
"I have been alone since this began," Vivienne said. "I will be alone when it ends. That is not a threat, Monsieur Chen. It is a *preference*."

"It's not a preference. It's a prison." Marcus didn't move. Didn't flinch. The blade was still at his throat and he could feel Elena vibrating with the effort of not lunging forward, could sense Sera's concern pulsing through the Bond like a distress signal from another building. "You've sealed yourself in a crystal box and you're watching yourself run out of air. You're doing to yourself what the apocalypse couldn't — choosing to disappear."
"Enough." The word was ice. The blade didn't waver.

But Vivienne's pulse, visible in the hollow of her throat, had quickened. And Marcus — who'd spent three weeks learning to read the hearts of women who'd built walls around themselves — saw the thing she was hiding behind all that pride.

Not arrogance. *Fear*. The same fear Sera had confessed on the rooftop. The same fear Junko carried in her scarred hands. The fear of being known and found wanting. The fear of reaching out and having no one reach back.

The Pride Barrier wasn't 98%. It was a shield made of pride, and underneath it was a woman who'd been abandoned by everyone she'd ever trusted and decided that the only way to never be left again was to never let anyone close enough to leave.

"We're going to Vale Industries Tower," Marcus said quietly. "There's a girl trapped in a panic room on the forty-second floor. She's got maybe ninety hours of air left. My partner — the woman I love — her sister is dying in that room, and we're going to get her out." He met Vivienne's green eyes. "You can kill me if you want. But if you don't, I'd like you to come with us."
The épée didn't move.
"You're asking me to *follow* you?" The incredulity in her voice was genuine — the sound of someone encountering a concept so foreign it barely registered as language.
"I'm asking you to stop starving to death in a crystal tower because you're too proud to admit you need people." He held her gaze. "Not demand. Not beg. Just... stop."
The silence stretched.
Elena stood frozen by the door. Sera's worry burned through the Bond like a beacon. Somewhere below, Junko waited on a department store roof with grey eyes and a thousand-yard stare.

And Vivienne Duchamps — French national fencing alternate, last survivor of the Crystal Tower, proudest woman in the apocalypse — held a blade to a stranger's throat and considered the impossible proposition of not being alone.
The épée lowered.
One centimeter. Two. Three.
Not surrender. Not acceptance. Just the smallest, most devastating retreat — the first step back from a war she'd been fighting against herself since the world ended.
"I will not ask for help," she said. Each word was dragged from somewhere deep and iron-clad. "I will not *beg*."
"I'm not asking you to."
"I will not be *pitied*."
"Wouldn't dream of it."

She stared at him. The blade trembled — just once, just barely — and then withdrew entirely. The épée swept to her side in a formal salute, the motion of a fencer acknowledging a touch, and Vivienne Duchamps sheathed the weapon in a cane-mounted scabbard Marcus hadn't noticed until now.

**[PRIDE BARRIER: 98% → 91%]**
**[QUEST PROGRESS: The Proud Heart — Target has retreated but not yielded. Continue approach.]**
**[Affection: 0 → 3]**
Three points. After everything — the blade at his throat, the hunger in her eyes, the crack in the crystal mask — three points.

But Marcus had learned something in three weeks of the Romance System: the first point was always the hardest. The first crack was always the deepest. And Vivienne Duchamps had just let a stranger sit in her study and call her afraid, and she hadn't killed him for it.
That wasn't nothing. That was everything.

"Sit down," Marcus said. "Elena's going to check your vitals, and you're going to let her, because I have food in my pack and I'm going to make you lunch and you're going to eat it like a human being instead of a monument."
Vivienne's eyes flashed. For one electrifying moment, Marcus thought she was going to draw the épée again.
Instead, she turned and walked to the window. Stood there, silhouetted against the prismatic light, one hand resting on the glass.
"I have been alone for twenty-three days," she said to the city. "Twenty-three days, and no one has come. No one has called. No one has so much as knocked."
Marcus reached into his pack and pulled out a System meal kit — nothing fancy, just rice and protein and vegetables in a self-heating container. The smell filled the sterile penthouse like a promise.
"We're here now," he said.
Vivienne didn't turn around. But her shoulders, which had been locked in architectural rigidity since they'd arrived, loosened by exactly one degree.
It wasn't much. But in the Crystal Tower, where everything was measured in fractals of refracted light, one degree was the difference between darkness and dawn.

The standoff wasn't over. The Pride Barrier still shimmered at ninety-one percent, and Vivienne Duchamps was still a starving woman with a sword and a refusal to bend. But Marcus had crossed the threshold — not her door, but her attention — and he wasn't leaving without her.
Not because the quest said so.
Because nobody should die alone in a crystal tower, too proud to scream.
